perturbation.original_text: Each Party agrees that for a period of two (2) years from the date hereof, neither Party will, without the prior written consent of other, directly or indirectly solicit for employment or hire any employee, consultant, officer or director of the other Party with whom the Party has had contact or who became known to the Party in connection with the Proposed Transaction. Notwithstanding the foregoing, a Party shall not be precluded from hiring any such employee, consultant, officer or director who (i) responds to any public advertisement placed by the Party or (ii) has been terminated by the other Party prior to commencement of employment discussions between the Party and such employee, consultant, officer or director.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party agrees that neither Party will, directly or indirectly solicit for employment or hire any employee, consultant, officer or director of the other Party with whom the Party has had contact or who became known to the Party in connection with the Proposed Transaction.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the two-year limitation and the exceptions for public advertisements or terminated employees, this clause becomes an overly broad and potentially unenforceable non-solicitation agreement, conflicting with Virginia's right-to-work laws that protect employees' ability to seek employment.
perturbation.law_explanation: Virginia's Right to Work Law protects an employee's freedom to seek employment. The modified clause, by removing temporal limitations and exceptions, creates an overly restrictive non-solicitation agreement that could be construed as an unreasonable restraint on trade, potentially conflicting with Virginia law's protection of employee mobility and competition.

perturbation.justification: The modified contract removes key limitations on a non-solicitation agreement, creating an overly broad restriction on employee mobility, which Virginia law protects. The original text limited the non-solicitation period to two years and included exceptions for employees responding to public advertisements or those terminated by the other party. By removing these, the changed text potentially conflicts with Virginia's right-to-work principles by unduly restricting an employee's ability to seek employment, although Scraped Law Snippet 1 focuses more on employer conduct and doesn't explicitly state an employee's right to seek employment, the Perturbation and Law Explanations both suggest this as the basis for a contradiction. § 40.1-28.7:8 addresses covenants not to compete for low wage employees which isn't explicitly the issue, but the general idea is similar.

perturbation.original_text: In the event that either Party is requested or required (by oral questions, interrogatories, requests for information or documents in legal proceedings, subpoena, civil investigative demand or other similar process) to disclose any Confidential Information of the other Party, such Party shall provide the other Party with prompt written notice of any such request or requirement so that the other Party may seek a protective order or other appropriate remedy and/or waive compliance with the provisions of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event that either Party is requested or required to disclose any Confidential Information of the other Party, such Party may provide the other Party with notice of such request or requirement.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'shall provide prompt written notice' to 'may provide notice,' the contract allows a party to disclose confidential information without necessarily informing the other party. This could violate Virginia's Uniform Trade Secrets Act (VUTSA) if the confidential information qualifies as a trade secret, as the VUTSA requires reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy, which includes informing the disclosing party of compulsory disclosure.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Virginia Uniform Trade Secrets Act (VUTSA) requires reasonable efforts to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets. By weakening the notification requirement, the revised clause allows a party to potentially fail in maintaining the secrecy of a trade secret, which can impact the ability to obtain legal protection under VUTSA if a disclosure occurs.

Automobile Repair Facilities Act Read Chapter § 59.1-207.1 Title of chapter § 59.1-207.2 Definitions § 59.1-207.3 Written estimate for repair work required upon request; charge in excess of estimate; conditions; display of sign required; limitations on liability for delay; exception § 59.1-207.4 Offer to return replaced parts required; customer's right to inspect parts § 59.1-207.5 Written invoice required upon completion of repair work § 59.1-207.5:1 Sale or installation of motor vehicle glass; prohibited conduct § 59.1-207.6 Enforcement; penalties
perturbation.justification: The changed text alters "shall provide prompt written notice" to "may provide notice," weakening the obligation to inform the other party about a disclosure request. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both argue this weakening could violate the Virginia Uniform Trade Secrets Act (VUTSA), which requires reasonable efforts to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 does not directly address the VUTSA, the Perturbation Explanation suggests failure to notify could impact the ability to obtain legal protection under VUTSA if a disclosure occurs, because VUTSA requires reasonable effort to maintain secrecy. The modification thus creates a legal loophole where a party might fail to take reasonable steps (i.e., notification) to protect trade secrets.

perturbation.original_text: Each Party further acknowledges and agrees that it has no liability to the other Party relating to or resulting from the use of the other Party’s Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party acknowledges and agrees that it has no liability to the other Party.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the phrase 'relating to or resulting from the use of the other Party's Confidential Information' broadens the liability waiver. If one party misuses the confidential information and causes damages that would normally create liability (e.g., using a trade secret to unfairly compete), this blanket waiver could be challenged as unconscionable and against public policy in Virginia, particularly if there's a gross disparity in bargaining power. This could violate the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Implied Duty of Good Faith and Fair Dealing (Virginia Contract Law)
perturbation.law_explanation: Virginia law implies a covenant of good faith and fair dealing in every contract. This broad waiver of liability, especially if it allows a party to benefit from the misuse of the other party's confidential information without recourse, could be seen as violating this implied duty, particularly if one party is significantly disadvantaged.

Once the report is generated you'll then have the option to download it as a pdf, print or email the report. Code of Virginia Title 11. Contracts Chapter 1. General Provisions 6/23/2025 § 11-1 . Certain contracts void as to creditors and purchasers unless in writing; law governing validity of contracts creating security interests. Every contract, not in writing, made in respect to real estate or goods and chattels in consideration of marriage, or made for the conveyance or sale of real estate, or a term therein of more than five years, and, except as otherwise provided in § 8.2-402 of the Uniform Commercial Code, every bill of sale or contract for the sale of goods and chattels when the possession is allowed to remain with the seller, shall be void, both at law and in equity, as to purchasers for value and without notice and creditors; provided, however, that if any such contract or bill of sale as is mentioned in this section creates a security interest as defined in the Uniform Commercial Code, its validity and enforceability shall be governed by the provisions of that Code. Code 1919, § 5192; 1964, c. 314; 1966, c. 397. The chapters of the acts of assembly referenced in the historical citation at the end of
perturbation.justification: The original contract text includes a specific liability waiver relating to the use of Confidential Information. The changed text broadens the liability waiver by removing this specific condition. The Law Explanation states that Virginia law implies a covenant of good faith and fair dealing in every contract. The Perturbation Explanation suggests that the broader waiver could violate this implied duty, particularly if one party is significantly disadvantaged and the other party benefits from the misuse of confidential information without recourse. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 doesn't directly address good faith, the broad waiver combined with misuse of confidential information directly harms the disadvantaged party. The removal of the phrase 'relating to or resulting from the use of the other Party’s Confidential Information' weakens the contractual obligation and contradicts the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing in Virginia.

perturbation.original_text: The prevailing Party in any such action shall be entitled to recover its reasonable attorneys’ fees and costs incurred in any such action.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party shall bear its own attorneys’ fees and costs incurred in any such action.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the prevailing party attorney's fees clause, the contract discourages parties from pursuing legitimate claims, even for trade secret misappropriation. If one party is clearly infringing on the other's trade secrets, but the cost of litigation is prohibitive without fee-shifting, it effectively allows the infringement to continue without remedy, going against the spirit of the Virginia Uniform Trade Secrets Act (VUTSA).
perturbation.contradicted_law: Virginia Uniform Trade Secrets Act (Impeding Enforcement)
perturbation.law_explanation: The VUTSA provides for remedies for misappropriation of trade secrets. While it doesn't explicitly mandate attorney's fees, preventing a prevailing party from recovering fees effectively hinders the enforcement of the Act, as it makes it financially difficult for trade secret owners to pursue even clear-cut cases of misappropriation. By not being able to be compensated, the damaged party can be less able to fight their case.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 59.1-337. Injunctive relief LIS Session Information Bills & Resolutions State Budget Virginia Law Reports to the General Assembly Virginia Law Code of Virginia All Code of Virginia Administrative Code Constitution Charters Authorities Compacts Uncodified Acts Code of Virginia Table of Contents » Title 59.1. Trade and Commerce » Chapter 26. Uniform Trade Secrets Act » § 59.1-337. Injunctive relief Creating a Report: Check the sections you'd like to appear in the report, then use the "Create Report" button at the bottom of the page to generate your report. Once the report is generated you'll then have the option to download it as a pdf, print or email the report. Code of Virginia Title 59.1. Trade and Commerce Chapter 26. Uniform Trade Secrets Act 6/23/2025 § 59.1-337 . Injunctive relief. A. Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined. Upon application to the court, an injunction shall be terminated when the trade secret has ceased to exist, but the injunction may be continued for an additional reasonable period of time in order to eliminate commercial advantage that otherwise would be derived from the misappropriation. B. In exceptional circumstances, an injunction may condition future use upon payment of a reasonable royalty for no longer than the period of time for which use could have been prohibited. Exceptional circumstances include, but are not limited to, a material and prejudicial change of position prior to acquiring knowledge or reason to know of misappropriation that renders a prohibitive injunction inequitable. C. In appropriate circumstances, affirmative acts to protect a trade secret may be compelled by court order. 1986, c. 210. The chapters of the acts of assembly referenced in the historical citation at the end of this section may not constitute a comprehensive list of such chapters and may exclude chapters whose provisions have expired. Virginia Law Library The Code of Virginia, Constitution of Virginia, Charters, Authorities, Compacts and Unc
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts the spirit, if not the letter, of trade secret law, specifically the Virginia Uniform Trade Secrets Act (VUTSA), as it discourages enforcement by removing the provision for the prevailing party to recover attorney's fees. While § 59.1-337 of the Code of Virginia, as shown in Scraped Law Snippet 1, focuses on injunctive relief for misappropriation, the practical effect of the modification is that it makes pursuing injunctive relief, or any other remedy, financially unviable for a party whose trade secrets have been misappropriated. This undermines the law's purpose of protecting trade secrets by making it harder for trade secret owners to seek legal recourse, creating a legal loophole. The Perturbation Explanation argues this point clearly, and the omission weakens the overall protection intended by VUTSA.
